chapters.write-only: You can never read it back

events.text: First, set a secret in env mode. The value lands as an environment variable inside the container. First writes need no ceremony — it is overwriting an existing secret that requires proof.
events.afterText: Set. The value will land in the container as an environment variable.

events.text: Now a file mode secret. File mode never puts the value in the container environment. It writes it to a file at slash run slash secrets, using Docker standard mechanism. Prefer this for anything sensitive.
events.afterText: Set as a file. The container reads it from /run/secrets, never as an env var.
events.prose: Now set a file-mode secret. File mode never exposes the value through the container environment; it writes the value to a file under /run/secrets using Docker's standard secrets mechanism. Prefer file mode for anything sensitive.

events.text: Let us list what we have. Names and modes only. The list never shows values, no matter who is asking.
events.afterText: Names and modes only. Values are never displayed.

events.text: Now the part that surprises people. The secret get command returns a digest, not the value. There is no flag that gives the plaintext back. That is the GitHub Actions model: write only by design.
events.afterText: Digest, not plaintext. The CLI won't show secrets to anyone.
events.prose: The secret get command returns a digest, not the value, and there is no flag to recover the plaintext. This follows the GitHub Actions model: secrets are write-only by design.

events.text: Lost the value? Do not peek. Rotate. The rotate-secret flag skips the precondition check, and the audit log marks the change as a deliberate rotation. Loud, intentional, safe.
events.afterText: Rotated. The audit log marks this as a deliberate replacement.
events.prose: If you lose track of a secret's value, rotate it rather than trying to recover it. The rotate-secret flag skips the precondition check and the audit log records the change as a deliberate rotation.

events.text: Time to prove the secrets actually work. Deploy the repo. The compose file consumes both: the env value through interpolation, the file value through a Docker secrets mount.
events.afterText: Services started. Both secrets were delivered at deploy time.
events.prose: Deploy the repo. The compose file consumes both secrets: the env value through interpolation, the file value through a Docker secrets mount.

events.text: Did the env secret arrive? Ask the container to print the variable. There is the value: postgres dot internal. The app really got it.
events.afterText: There's the value. The app really received it.
events.prose: Print the variable inside the container: postgres.internal. The env-mode secret reached the app at deploy time.

events.text: And the file secret? Read it from slash run slash secrets inside the container. There it is, the rotated value. The app reads the plaintext; it is only the tooling that refuses to show it to you.
events.afterText: The rotated value, live in the container. Write-only applies to humans, not to your app.
events.prose: Read /run/secrets/stripe_key inside the container: the rotated value is mounted there. The app gets the plaintext; only the CLI refuses to display it.

events.text: Now the punchline. We will fork the repo. The fork is a byte-for-byte copy of the parent encrypted image.
events.afterText: Fork created. A byte-for-byte copy of the encrypted image.
events.prose: Fork the repo. The fork is a byte-for-byte copy of the parent's encrypted image.

events.text: But when we list the fork secrets: empty. No Stripe key, no database password, no API token. The fork cannot pretend to be you. That is why clone production, break nothing actually works.
events.afterText: Empty. The fork starts with no secrets, by design.
events.prose: Listing the fork's secrets returns an empty set: no Stripe key, no database password, no API token. The fork cannot impersonate the parent, which is what makes cloning production safe.

events.text: One more proof. Try to start the fork with the parent compose. It fails: the bind source path for the Stripe key does not exist under the fork. Production credentials never follow a fork, not even by accident.
events.afterText: Bind source path does not exist. The missing secret blocks the start, and that failure is the feature.
events.prose: Starting the fork with the parent's compose fails: the secret file does not exist under the fork's network ID, so Docker refuses the bind mount. Production credentials never follow a fork.

narrations.text: Time to put real credentials in a place forks can't reach! We'll set two secrets, deploy the app and prove it really receives them, then fork the repo and watch the fork come up empty handed. It can't even start.

Most teams drop a dot env file into the repo because it's the obvious move. The problem is the fork inherits a byte-for-byte copy of the image, so your sandbox boots with the production Stripe key and starts charging real customer cards.
The CLI follows the GitHub Actions model: get returns a digest, never the value. If you lose track of a secret, you don't peek, you rotate, and the audit log records it as a deliberate change.

Here's the payoff. The fork shares the parent's encrypted data, but the secret store is scoped per repo, so the fork lists empty, can't interpolate any of your production keys, and can't even start the parent's compose.
And that's secrets locked down where forks can't follow. See you in the next one!
